Color Referent Image Inventory

Coverage, acquisition pipeline, and filtering decisions for images

Author

Thomas Brochhagen

Published

March 4, 2026

Overview

This report documents the image acquisition pipeline. The goal is to obtain visual representations of the unique concepts that, cross-linguistic, appear as sources; to then extract mean saliency maps from these images, and then recast the results as distributions over Munsell chips for comparison with the World Color Survey (WCS). This report is about the first step: finding images to represent the concepts.

I’m also using this to track the pre-processing decisions I took and their potential consequences/biases, since those choices directly affect what color signals end up in the saliency maps. I tried to get at most 20 images per source for now.

The pipeline:

Step Script Description
1-2 04-fetch-images.py Download + bbox-crop images from curated datasets

Concept list

Code 
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)

df_glosses <- read_csv("../data/unique_glosses.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
cat(sprintf("Unique cleaned glosses: %d\n", length(unique(df_glosses$cleangloss))))
Unique cleaned glosses: 617

Top 20 most frequent sources across languages:

Code 
df_cgloss <- read_csv("../data/color-cgloss.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

df_cgloss |>
  count(source, sort = TRUE) |>
  slice_head(n = 20) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(source, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4e9af1") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Number of color terms using this concept as source",
       title = "Top 20 sources across languages") +
  theme_minimal()

Image sources

So far, images are drawn from curated, research-grade datasets (e.g., not crawled from WikiMedia). Sources 1–4 use bounding-box annotations (squares that identify the target object in an image) so every saved image is cropped to the annotated object (plus 10% padding), giving concept-centred frames. This may be better suited to saliency analysis. Sources 5–6 lack per-instance bboxes but are already organism- or object-centred by design, so full images are saved after a minimum-size check. I expect these to be a little noisier.

# Dataset License Image pool Bbox? Sources covered
1 LVIS v1 CC BY 4.0 COCO train2017 (~118 k images) Yes — [x, y, w, h] px 22
2 COCO instances CC BY 4.0 Same pool Yes — [x, y, w, h] px 3
3 OpenImages V7 Various CC ~1.7 M images (CVDF S3) Yes — [XMin, XMax, YMin, YMax] norm. 12
4 VAW (Visual Attributes in the Wild) Research use Visual Genome (~108 k images, Stanford) Yes — [left, top, w, h] px 59
5 iNaturalist CC0 / CC-BY / CC-BY-SA API (~180 M observations) No — full image 29 (after manual exclusions)
6 ImageNet-21k Research use Local download required (~14 M images) No — full image TBD (requires local data)

Not used for now:

  • COCO-Stuff: background categories (sky, grass, snow…) have bboxes that span large scene regions, making cropping uninformative for isolating the concept. I’m not sure about this choice because some of these are used as sources. But I first excluded this data to get a working pipeline with clearer cases off the ground. [DECISION TO CIRCLE BACK TO]
  • Wikimedia Commons: no bbox annotations and highly variable image quality. I tried this and it was messy. I could still source from Wikimedia and then have a human sort through it but then we need clearer guidelines for image selection. [DECISION TO CIRCLE BACK TO]

Source access details

OpenImages — images fetched directly from the CVDF public S3 bucket over HTTP (no AWS CLI required): https://s3.amazonaws.com/open-images-dataset/train/{id}.jpg. The per-concept annotation index is built once from oidv6-train-annotations-bbox.csv and cached as data/img-data/oi_index.parquet for fast subsequent runs.

VAW — images fetched from Stanford’s public Visual Genome hosting: https://cs.stanford.edu/people/rak248/VG_100K/{id}.jpg (with VG_100K_2 as fallback). For non-commercial research use.

iNaturalist — photos fetched on-demand from the iNaturalist API (https://api.inaturalist.org/v1/observations) filtered to photo_license=cc0,cc-by,cc-by-sa and quality_grade=research. The square thumbnail URL returned by the API is rewritten to large (max 1024 px) before download. API rate limit: ≤1 request/second.

ImageNet-21k — requires a local copy of the dataset. Provide the root directory with --imagenet21k-dir PATH. Concepts are matched to synsets via words.txt (synset_id → comma-separated names bundled with the dataset).

Filtering decisions and potential biases

This section documents the automated filters applied during image acquisition. These choices should be kept in mind for down-stream analyses.

F1 — Dataset source filter

Decision: six sources are used in priority order: LVIS → COCO-instances → OpenImages V7 → VAW → iNaturalist → ImageNet-21k.

Rationale: each source is either openly CC-licensed or available for non-commercial research use and provides either bbox annotations (sources 1–4) or organism/object-centred photographs (sources 5–6).

Bias:

  • COCO/LVIS/OpenImages were collected with English-language queries and are biased toward Western, anglophone visual culture [@lin2014microsoft; @gupta2019lvis].

  • VAW (Visual Genome) shares the same English-centric Flickr image pool.

  • iNaturalist is biased toward North American and European biodiversity hotspots where the app has the most users; tropical and Southern-hemisphere taxa are under-represented.

  • ImageNet-21k was sourced from web image search and inherits similar cultural biases.

  • Concepts from non-Western or non-commercial contexts (egbo powder, dawa dawa fruit, boiled taraγ) have close to zero coverage across all sources.

F2 — Minimum crop size: 150 × 150 px (for images with bounding boxes)

Decision: crops smaller than 150 × 150 pixels after padding are discarded.

Rationale: very small crops are too low-resolution for reliable saliency computation.

Bias?

  • May excludes distant or small instances of objects; which is to say that it favors large, prominent referents. Not sure if this is an issue but worth keeping in mind.

F3 — Maximum crop-to-image ratio: 0.75 (for images with bounding boxes)

Decision: crops whose area exceeds 75% of the original image area are discarded.

Rationale: when the bounding box fills nearly the entire image the annotation often corresponds to a studio/product shot, taxidermied specimen, toy, or carved replica rather than a natural in-situ instance. These would contribute incorrect color signals (e.g., a wooden deer carving is the wrong color for “deer”).

Bias?

  • May exclude close-up macro photography of naturally photogenic objects (insects, flowers, coins) that genuinely fill the frame.

  • Slightly favours mid-range distances where the object is prominent but surrounded by natural context.

F4 — Sort by descending bbox area (COCO / LVIS)

Decision: within a category, annotations are sorted largest-bbox-first; the first N distinct images are selected.

Rationale: larger annotated objects tend to be the intended focus of the image and yield higher-resolution crops.

Bias?

  • May preferentially sample prominent foreground instances and under-represent the same object at varied sizes or distances.

F5 — OpenImages quality flags

Decision: annotations are sorted by the number of quality flags set to 0 (best quality first): IsOccluded, IsTruncated, IsGroupOf, IsDepiction.

Rationale: non-occluded, non-truncated, individually-annotated, photographic instances provide the most unambiguous color signal.

Potential biases (though I think these are very clear cut):

  • IsGroupOf = 0: prefers individual instances over groups/crowds; may under-represent objects that are typically seen in aggregates (coins in a pile, grapes in a bunch, etc.).

  • IsDepiction = 0: prefers photographs over paintings, logos, or illustrations. For concepts like rose, this means predominantly photographic roses rather than stylised representations. Note: this flag does not catch all depictions; see manual curation entry #1 (honeycomb) where an image of a printed book cover passed this filter.

  • IsOccluded = 0 / IsTruncated = 0: prefers fully visible objects; may under-represent naturally partial views (e.g., a deer partly behind a tree).

F6 — Bounding-box padding: 10%

Decision: each crop is expanded by 10% of the bbox dimensions on each side before saving.

Rationale: saliency models benefit from a small amount of surrounding context for edge detection and figure-ground separation. Without any padding, a tightly clipped object would look unnatural to the model.

Bias? - Introduces some background context into each crop, which may attract saliency away from the referent itself. The 10% value is a relatively arbitrary choice; larger padding reintroduces more background noise. I don’t expect the difference between a few percentage points to matter but I haven’t tested. Not even sure if this does much but I’ve seen this choice in some papers.

F7 — Exact / near-exact string matching for category lookup

Decision: concept glosses are matched to dataset categories by exact lowercase string matching, plus underscore-normalisation and synonym lookup for LVIS. iNaturalist additionally performs server-side taxon-name search (scientific and common names), which provides some implicit synonym coverage for biological taxa.

Rationale: fuzzy matching would risk cross-category contamination (e.g., matching “lime” to “lemon”). We may want to do this for some sources though… so [DECISION TO CIRCLE BACK TO]

Consequence:

  • Zero coverage for about 535 sources (87%) because they do not match any dataset category across all six sources. These concepts are typically culturally specific, abstract, or biologically precise (cassumunar ginger, beautiful, vitiligo).
  • This is the largest source of selection bias / coverage loss in the pipeline: the concepts for which we can compute saliency maps are a non-random subset biased toward globally common, photographable objects. This is currently a choice to get the pipeline working but [DECISION TO CIRCLE BACK TO]

F8 — iNaturalist: research-grade filter

Decision: only observations with quality_grade=research are used.

Rationale: iNaturalist assigns “research grade” when ≥2/3 of community identifications agree on a taxon. This ensures the taxon label is reliable.

Consequence:

  • Preferentially retains common, easily-identified taxa in well-surveyed regions. Rare or visually ambiguous species are under-represented because they rarely achieve research grade.
  • Also, note that iNaturalist photos are taken by citizen scientists in the field, so they tend to show organisms in natural habitat. This is good for our purposes. However, photo quality varies more than in professionally curated datasets. I saw that a few images of boars are in black and white. So maybe that should be checked for. [DECISION TO CIRCLE BACK TO, at least to exclude black and white]

F9 — iNaturalist: open photo license filter

Decision: only photos with photo_license ∈ {cc0, cc-by, cc-by-sa} are downloaded.

Rationale: these licenses permit academic redistribution and reuse.

Summary table

Code 
tribble(
  ~Filter, ~Parameter, ~Effect, ~Bias_direction,
  "F1 — Dataset source",      "LVIS > COCO > OI > VAW > iNat > IN21k",  "Six curated sources in priority order", "Western/anglophone bias; iNat skewed to N. America/Europe",
  "F2 — Min crop size",       "150 × 150 px",           "Discard tiny crops",               "Favours large, prominent instances",
  "F3 — Max crop fraction",   "≤ 75% of image area",    "Discard whole-scene bbox",         "Excludes studio shots & replicas; may exclude macro photography",
  "F4 — Sort by bbox area",   "Largest first",          "Select prominent instances",       "Favours foreground, large specimens",
  "F5 — OI quality flags",    "Prefer 0 on all flags",  "Prefer unoccluded photos",         "Prefers isolated, photographic, individual instances",
  "F6 — Bbox padding",        "10%",                    "Add context around object",        "Adds background; affects saliency model boundary detection",
  "F7 — String matching",     "Exact + synonyms",       "Determine which concepts get images", "Zero coverage for most concepts (culturally specific, abstract)",
  "F8 — iNat quality grade",  "research only",          "Community-verified IDs only",      "Favours common, easily-ID'd taxa in well-surveyed regions",
  "F9 — iNat photo license",  "cc0 / cc-by / cc-by-sa","Open-license photos only",         "--",
) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Filter", "Parameter", "Effect", "Bias direction"),
    caption = "Automated filtering decisions and their bias implications"
  )
Automated filtering decisions and their bias implications
Filter Parameter Effect Bias direction
F1 — Dataset source LVIS > COCO > OI > VAW > iNat > IN21k Six curated sources in priority order Western/anglophone bias; iNat skewed to N. America/Europe
F2 — Min crop size 150 × 150 px Discard tiny crops Favours large, prominent instances
F3 — Max crop fraction ≤ 75% of image area Discard whole-scene bbox Excludes studio shots & replicas; may exclude macro photography
F4 — Sort by bbox area Largest first Select prominent instances Favours foreground, large specimens
F5 — OI quality flags Prefer 0 on all flags Prefer unoccluded photos Prefers isolated, photographic, individual instances
F6 — Bbox padding 10% Add context around object Adds background; affects saliency model boundary detection
F7 — String matching Exact + synonyms Determine which concepts get images Zero coverage for most concepts (culturally specific, abstract)
F8 — iNat quality grade research only Community-verified IDs only Favours common, easily-ID’d taxa in well-surveyed regions
F9 — iNat photo license cc0 / cc-by / cc-by-sa Open-license photos only –

Coverage results

Code 
manifest_path <- "../data/images/manifest.csv"

if (file.exists(manifest_path)) {
  manifest <- read_csv(manifest_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  total   <- nrow(manifest)
  any_img <- sum(manifest$n_images > 0)
  zero    <- sum(manifest$n_images == 0)
  full    <- sum(manifest$n_images >= 20)

  cat(sprintf("Concepts processed:              %d\n", total))
  cat(sprintf("  ≥ 1 image:                     %d (%.0f%%)\n", any_img, 100 * any_img / total))
  cat(sprintf("  ≥ 20 images (target):          %d (%.0f%%)\n", full,    100 * full    / total))
  cat(sprintf("  0 images (no curated match):   %d (%.0f%%)\n", zero,    100 * zero    / total))
} else {
  cat("Manifest not yet available — fetch still running.\n")
}
Concepts processed:              615
  ≥ 1 image:                     107 (17%)
  ≥ 20 images (target):          63 (10%)
  0 images (no curated match):   508 (83%)
Code 
if (file.exists(manifest_path)) {
  manifest |>
    filter(n_images > 0) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = n_images)) +
    geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "#4e9af1", color = "white") +
    labs(x = "Number of images downloaded", y = "Number of concepts",
         title = "Image coverage per concept (concepts with ≥ 1 image)") +
    theme_minimal()
}

Zero-coverage concepts (sample)

Code 
if (file.exists(manifest_path)) {
  manifest |>
    filter(n_images == 0) |>
    slice_head(n = 30) |>
    pull(concept) |>
    paste(collapse = ", ") |>
    cat()
}
1000 peseta note, accept, acid reflux, air, albino, alcoholic watermelon juice beverage, alive, allow, and, and maui, annatto, anoint, appear, apple bladder-nut, appoint, appreciate, ash, baby sling, bad, banana bunch, bay-coloured reindeer, be, be able, be alive, be silent, bead, bear young, beautiful, become taut, begin

Image examples

  • Rose (OpenImages)
  • Monkey (LVIS)
  • Banana (COCO + LVIS)
  • Lettuce (VAW)
  • Deer (LVIS + OpenImages)
Code 
imgs <- list.files("../data/images/rose", "img_.*\\.jpg", full.names = TRUE)
if (length(imgs) > 0) knitr::include_graphics(imgs[1:min(3, length(imgs))])
Code 
imgs <- list.files("../data/images/monkey", "img_.*\\.jpg", full.names = TRUE)
if (length(imgs) > 0) knitr::include_graphics(imgs[1:min(3, length(imgs))])
Code 
imgs <- list.files("../data/images/banana", "img_.*\\.jpg", full.names = TRUE)
if (length(imgs) > 0) knitr::include_graphics(imgs[1:min(3, length(imgs))])
Code 
imgs <- list.files("../data/images/lettuce", "img_.*\\.jpg", full.names = TRUE)
if (length(imgs) > 0) knitr::include_graphics(imgs[1:min(3, length(imgs))])

Note: includes a minority of non-living instances (taxidermy, mounted trophies) due to annotation noise in the source dataset — see F3 above.

Code 
imgs <- list.files("../data/images/deer", "img_.*\\.jpg", full.names = TRUE)
if (length(imgs) > 0) knitr::include_graphics(imgs[1:min(4, length(imgs))])

Manual curation log

The following decisions were made by hand after looking at some downloaded images.

# Concept Source Matched taxon Problem Action Implication
1 honeycomb OpenImages — “honeycomb” category systematically returns bee swarms, bee hive frames obscured by bees, wasp/paper nests (grey hexagonal cells — wrong color), and printed depictions. None of 20 images showed an isolated honeycomb structure. Folder deleted; excluded via --skip honeycomb. Zero images.
2 white iNaturalist Pierinae (butterfly subfamily “Whites”) “white” is a color term; matched via plural “Whites” to unrelated butterfly group. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. 5 VAW images retained. 5 images remain (VAW). iNaturalist excluded.
3 brilliant iNaturalist Heliodoxa (hummingbirds “Brilliants”) “brilliant” means bright/shining; matched to hummingbird genus via plural “Brilliants”. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.
4 permit iNaturalist Trachinotus falcatus (Permit fish) “permit” is likely a color term meaning authorization/allowance; matched to a marine fish species. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.
5 leucoma iNaturalist Leucoma (moth genus) “leucoma” refers to a white corneal opacity (eye condition); matched to a moth genus sharing the Latin root. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.
6 ochre iNaturalist Trapezites (butterflies “Ochres”) “ochre” is a color pigment; matched via plural “Ochres” to a butterfly genus. The butterflies are ochre-colored but the match is coincidental. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.
7 mother-of-pearl iNaturalist Salamis (butterflies “Mother-of-Pearls”) “mother-of-pearl” refers to iridescent shell material; matched via plural to an iridescent butterfly genus. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.
8 stock iNaturalist Matthiola (flowers “Stock”) “stock” is likely used as a color term; matched to the ornamental flower genus. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.
9 bead iNaturalist Curio (succulents “Beads”) “bead” refers to a decorative object; matched via plural “Beads” to a succulent genus. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.
10 yellow ochre iNaturalist Trapezites luteus (“Yellow Ochre” butterfly) Same genus as entry #6 (ochre). The species name matches the color pigment but the referent is a butterfly. iNat images deleted; added to INAT_MANUAL_EXCLUSIONS. Zero images.

This log should be updated whenever images are manually reviewed and modified.

Next steps

Extending coverage

Some options to extend coverage:

  1. ImageNet-21k local data — with a local copy of the dataset, I expect coverage would expand at least for everyday objects and many biological taxa. Download from image-net.org (registration required) or the Hugging Face mirror; provide the root directory with --imagenet21k-dir PATH.

  2. Relax string matching — use WordNet synonyms, Concepticon concept mappings, or embedding-based fuzzy matching to link more glosses to dataset categories. Risk: higher false-positive rate.

  3. OpenImages V7 extended — the boxable set covers ~600 categories; there may be additional matches with alternate spellings or synonym lookup.

  4. Manual curation — for culturally specific items (egbo powder, boiled taraγ), a small hand-collected set is likely more reliable than any automated pipeline.
